title: Working with the part that believes it does not deserve love
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

htmlContent:
The part that believes it does not deserve love

I know this part. I've sat with it in my own body for years. It's the voice that says "you had this coming" when something goes wrong. It's the knot in your stomach when someone offers you genuine kindness. It's the reflex to deflect, to downplay, to disappear when love actually shows up.

This part doesn't announce itself with dramatic declarations. It whispers. It's the quiet assumption underneath everything. The belief that if people really knew you, they'd leave. That your needs are too much. That you have to earn love through performance, through usefulness, through being small and agreeable and never asking for anything.

Here's the thing. This part isn't wrong about what it learned. It learned something real. Somewhere along the way, probably very early, you received the message that who you are is not enough. Maybe it was spoken directly. Maybe it was communicated through neglect, through absence, through the way your caregiver's face went flat when you needed comfort. The part took that information in and built a whole system around it. A survival system.

Richard Schwartz, who developed Internal Family Systems (IFS) therapy, calls these parts "protectors." They're not bad. They're not broken. They're doing a job. This part that believes you don't deserve love? It's protecting you from the pain of hoping for love and being disappointed. From the devastation of reaching out and being rejected. From the unbearable vulnerability of actually letting someone in and then losing them.

I've worked with this part in myself for a long time. And I've watched it show up in clients, in friends, in strangers who write to me after reading something that hits too close to home. It's one of the most common parts in anyone with complex trauma. And it's one of the most painful.

Sit with that for a second. Notice what just happened in your body when I named it.

Where this part comes from

This part doesn't appear out of nowhere. It's formed in relationship. Specifically, in relationships where love was conditional, unpredictable, or absent. Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, talks about how the body stores these relational wounds. The part that believes it doesn't deserve love isn't just a thought. It's a full-body experience. A collapse in the chest. A heaviness in the shoulders. A numbness in the hands. A turning away.

When you grow up with emotional neglect, you learn that your inner world doesn't matter. That your feelings are an inconvenience. That the only way to get connection is to attend to someone else's needs while pretending you don't have any. This is how the part gets reinforced. Every time you reached out and got nothing back, the part said "see? this is what happens." Every time you tried to express a need and were shamed for it, the part said "you should know better."

Janina Fisher, who writes about structural dissociation, describes how trauma splits the self into parts that carry different memories and beliefs. The part that believes it doesn't deserve love is often what she calls a "survival part." It adapted to an impossible situation by concluding that the problem was you. Because if the problem was you, then maybe you could fix it. Maybe if you tried harder, became better, made yourself smaller, earned love more perfectly, you could finally be acceptable.

This is the tragedy of the part. It's trying so hard. It's working overtime to keep you safe from the pain of hoping. But the cost is enormous. You can't receive love when this part is running the show. You can't let it in. Every compliment gets filtered through the belief that you don't deserve it. Every act of kindness gets explained away. Every moment of connection gets sabotaged before it can become real.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker's work directly addresses the inner critic and the shame that fuels this part. He names the dynamic clearly and offers practical ways to work with it.

How to actually work with this part

Working with this part doesn't mean getting rid of it. That's a common misunderstanding. People come to therapy or self-work wanting to annihilate the part that believes they don't deserve love. They want it gone. Dead. Erased. But that's just another form of rejection. You can't heal rejection by rejecting another part of yourself.

The work is relationship. It's turning toward this part with curiosity instead of contempt. It's asking "what are you afraid would happen if you didn't believe this?" instead of arguing with it. It's sitting with the part and feeling what it feels in the body, without trying to change it or fix it or talk it out of its belief.

I'll be honest with you. This is hard. Really hard. The part doesn't trust you. Why would it? You've probably been fighting it for years. Calling it names. Trying to override it with affirmations that felt hollow. Pushing it away with spiritual bypassing or toxic positivity. The part has good reason to keep its defenses up.

But here's what I've found. When you slow down and actually listen, the part has a story to tell. A story that needs to be heard. A story that might make you cry. A story about a child who tried so hard to be loved and couldn't figure out why it wasn't working. A story about a child who concluded that the problem must be them, because that was the only explanation that left any hope at all.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link) by Richard Schwartz. It explains the IFS model in accessible language and helps you understand how to relate to your parts with compassion rather than fear.

The goal isn't to convince the part that you deserve love. That's a cognitive approach, and this part doesn't respond to logic. It responds to presence. To witnessing. To someone finally seeing the pain it's been carrying and not running away. When the part feels seen, it can start to relax. Not all at once. Not completely. But a little. Enough to let a crack of light in.

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link) by Janina Fisher. Her work on structural dissociation is incredibly helpful for understanding why parts form and how to work with them systematically.

What happens when the part starts to shift

I don't want to give you false hope. This part doesn't just disappear. It doesn't transform overnight into a part that feels completely worthy and open to love. That's not how healing works. But something does change. The part becomes less rigid. Less absolute. It starts to allow other experiences in alongside its belief.

You might notice moments where you receive a compliment and something different happens. You still feel the reflex to deflect, but there's a tiny pause. A space where you could choose to just say "thank you" instead of arguing. You might notice moments where someone offers help and you let them, even though it feels uncomfortable. You might notice moments where you feel sad instead of numb, and that sadness is actually a relief because it means you're feeling something real.

This is the part softening. It's not gone. It's still there. But it's no longer running the entire show. It's taking a step back and letting other parts have a turn. Parts that can receive. Parts that can trust. Parts that can risk being seen.

I've seen this happen in my own life. The part that believed I didn't deserve love still shows up. It still has opinions. It still tries to protect me. But I've learned to recognize it. To thank it for its work. To let it know that I'm here now, and I can handle things differently. And slowly, over years, the part has started to believe me. Not completely. But enough.

For understanding emotional neglect specifically, a helpful resource is Running on Empty (paid link) by Jonice Webb. It names the invisible wound of growing up without emotional attunement, which is often the root of the belief that you don't deserve love.

The ongoing practice

Working with this part isn't a one-time thing. It's a practice. A daily, sometimes hourly, practice of noticing when the part is active and choosing a different response. It's the practice of saying "I notice this part believes I don't deserve love" instead of collapsing into that belief. It's the practice of feeling the shame in your body and breathing into it instead of numbing out or distracting yourself.

Dan Siegel talks about integration as the key to healing. Integration means connecting different parts of the self so they can work together instead of fighting each other. When you integrate this part, it doesn't disappear. It becomes part of a larger system. A system that includes the part that believes you don't deserve love, but also includes the part that knows you do, and the part that's learning to hold both at the same time.

This is the real work. Not achieving some permanent state of worthiness. Not erasing the part. But learning to live with the contradiction. Learning to hold the belief and the doubt together. Learning to let love in even when it terrifies you. Learning to trust that you can handle the disappointment if it doesn't work out.

You can do this. I know you can. Because you're still here. You're still trying. You're still reading words about healing, which means some part of you hasn't given up. That part matters too. That part deserves your attention and your gratitude. That part is the reason you're still moving forward.

The part that believes you don't deserve love isn't your enemy. It's your wounded protector. It's the part that took the hit so you could survive. And it deserves your compassion, not your contempt. When you can offer that compassion, something shifts. Not everything. But something. And sometimes that something is enough to make all the difference.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link)
  • No Bad Parts (paid link)
  • Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link)
  • Running on Empty (paid link)
